title: Cages of Fear, a Gamebook by Joe Ward
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YXqYOapYqYc

Daniel (@dungeondive) admits up front that his love of gamebooks has cooled over the years — he keeps measuring them against Fabled Lands, still his reigning king of the genre for its open-ended questing — but Cages of Fear by Joe Ward, recommended in the Dungeon Dive Facebook group, genuinely won him over. His neat framing: if Escape the Dark Castle feels like a gamebook's pages torn out and turned into cards, Cages of Fear feels like someone gathered those cards back into a book.

You wake in a prison cell and fight your way out of a sprawling dungeon. Pick one of three classes (footman, magister, brigand), track damage/intellect/health with one weapon, amulet and armour equipped, and level via an experience track. Rather than choosing paragraphs, you clear a room then roll a d3 to advance that many "rooms" forward — a roguelike escape run where you chase survival, not story. Combat is bare Fighting Fantasy (opposed rolls, highest wins), and his one real wish is a luck-style stat to soften the dice, since a charnel rat can fluke-kill you instantly.

The star, though, is the art: Ward illustrates every single page — a glut of glorious skeletons — and that, with the snappy card-like layout, is what makes him reach for it over wordier gamebooks.

----

Do you want a gamebook rich in prose and branching story, or a lean, art-driven survival run like this — and is Fabled Lands still the one to beat?
